threats.title: MorphoBlueConnector._getPositionTVL Adds Borrow Amount Instead of Subtracting, Inflating Vault TVL
threats.hypothesis_description: MorphoBlueConnector._getPositionTVL computes TVL as `supplyAmount + borrowAmount + collateralInLoanToken`. The borrowAmount should be SUBTRACTED, not added. Additionally, when `borrow()` is called, borrowed tokens are sent to the connector and registered as held tokens via `_updateTokenInRegistry`. TVLHelper then counts: (1) the borrowed tokens as a held token balance, AND (2) the Morpho position TVL which erroneously adds borrowAmount. This results in TVL inflation of approximately 2x the borrow value, allowing share price manipulation, excessive performance fee extraction, and unfair deposit/withdrawal pricing.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code explicitly adds borrowAmount positively in the formula. This is a direct arithmetic error in a critical TVL calculation path that affects share pricing for all users.
preconditions: MorphoBlueConnector is an active connector with a Morpho position
Manager calls borrow() to borrow assets from Morpho Blue
TVL is subsequently queried for share calculations

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: borrowAmount added instead of subtracted in TVL formula
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: supplyAmount + borrowAmount + collateralValue should be supplyAmount - borrowAmount + collateralValue; additionally borrowed tokens are double-counted as held token positions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No validation that Morpho position TVL formula is consistent with standard lending TVL = collateral - debt

analysis_questions: What is the exact magnitude of TVL inflation for a given borrow amount?
Can a manager strategically borrow to inflate TVL and extract performance fees?
Does the double-counting with held token positions compound the inflation?
validation_requirements: Trace a complete borrow->TVL calculation path showing inflated value
Calculate the maximum possible TVL inflation relative to actual assets
Verify that borrowed tokens appear in BOTH held token positions AND Morpho position TVL

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager borrows X tokens from Morpho Blue. Vault TVL increases by ~2X (held tokens + incorrectly-added borrow in position TVL). Manager calls recordProfitForFee, extracting performance fees on phantom profits. Alternatively, new depositors receive fewer shares than deserved due to inflated TVL.
prerequisites: Active Morpho Blue market with available liquidity

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: TVL inflated by 2x borrow amount; performance fees on phantom profits; unfair share pricing for all depositors/withdrawers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix; accumulated fee extraction is irreversible

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for Morpho borrow impact on vault TVL

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Any borrow amount amplifies the issue
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing on-chain TVL with expected values

threats.title: Registry onlyVaultMaintainer Modifier Uses OR Instead of AND, Requiring Both Maintainer AND Emergency Roles
threats.hypothesis_description: The `onlyVaultMaintainer` modifier in Registry.sol uses `||` in its revert condition: `if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false)`. By De Morgan's law, this passes ONLY when the caller is BOTH the vault maintainer AND holds the global EMERGENCY_ROLE. All other vault modifiers (onlyVaultGoverner, onlyVaultMaintainerWithoutTimeLock) correctly use `&&`, passing when EITHER condition is met. This locks `addConnector`, `updateConnectorTrustedTokens`, and `removeTrustedPosition` unless the maintainer address also holds EMERGENCY_ROLE.
threats.investigation_rationale: Comparison with other modifiers in the same contract clearly shows the inconsistency. The `onlyVaultGoverner` uses `&&` in its revert condition, which is the standard OR-either-role pattern. The onlyVaultMaintainer uses `||`, which is mathematically different.
threats.config_evidence: Deployment script updateRegistry.ts shows separate addresses used but also grants EMERGENCY_ROLE to the same address, possibly as a workaround for this bug

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false) — uses || where && is intended, compare with onlyVaultGoverner at lines 38-41 which uses &&

preconditions: Vault maintainer address does NOT hold the global EMERGENCY_ROLE
Caller attempts to use addConnector, updateConnectorTrustedTokens, or removeTrustedPosition

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Boolean OR (||) in revert condition instead of AND (&&)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: onlyVaultMaintainer requires BOTH roles instead of EITHER, unlike all other vault modifiers
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative path for vault maintainer without emergency role

analysis_questions: Is the vault maintainer expected to hold EMERGENCY_ROLE in production?
Are addConnector and updateConnectorTrustedTokens reachable in production?
Does the deployment script work around this by granting EMERGENCY_ROLE to the maintainer?
validation_requirements: Confirm by testing that a maintainer-only address (without EMERGENCY_ROLE) cannot call addConnector
Compare with onlyVaultGoverner to confirm the intended pattern

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: In a production deployment where vault maintainer and emergency are separate addresses (as intended by the governance design), the maintainer cannot add connectors, update trusted tokens, or remove positions. This blocks vault administration and prevents the vault from adapting to changing DeFi conditions.
prerequisites: Vault maintainer address differs from EMERGENCY_ROLE holder

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Vault becomes unable to update connectors or trusted tokens, potentially trapping funds in deprecated protocols
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires granting EMERGENCY_ROLE to maintainer as workaround, or code fix

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for maintainer-only access to addConnector without emergency role

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Easily detectable by attempting to call addConnector as vault maintainer without emergency role

threats.title: Registry Swap-and-Pop Position Removal Corrupts isPositionUsed Mapping via Wrong Key Computation
threats.hypothesis_description: When removing a holding position via `updateHoldingPosition`, the swap-and-pop logic recomputes the key for the swapped element using `vault.holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector`. However, the original key was computed as `keccak256(abi.encode(msg.sender, _positionId, _data))` where `msg.sender` is the connector that created the position. For token positions (positionTypeId=0), `calculatorConnector` is the AccountingManager but the creating connector is a BaseConnector derivative. This mismatch means the `isPositionUsed` mapping for the moved element points to the wrong key, corrupting position tracking.
threats.investigation_rationale: In BaseConnector._updateTokenInRegistry, positionId uses accountingManager as calculatorConnector, but msg.sender (the connector) is used for the holdingPositionId. When a different position is removed and this one is swapped, the recomputed key won't match the original.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Swap-and-pop recomputes key using vault.holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector but original key used msg.sender; these differ for token positions where calculatorConnector=accountingManager and creator=connector

preconditions: At least 2 holding positions exist in the vault
The removed position is not the last element in the array
The swapped position's calculatorConnector differs from the connector that created it

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: vault.isPositionUsed[keccak256(abi.encode(vault.holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector, ...))] uses calculatorConnector instead of the original msg.sender
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: For token positions, calculatorConnector is accountingManager but creator connector is a BaseConnector derivative
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No field in HoldingPI explicitly stores the original key, and the code assumes calculatorConnector==creator

analysis_questions: How frequently does calculatorConnector differ from the creating connector?
What is the impact on subsequent getHoldingPositionIndex lookups?
Can corrupted isPositionUsed lead to duplicate positions or prevent removal?
validation_requirements: Create a scenario with multiple token positions from different connectors
Remove one position and verify the isPositionUsed mapping for the swapped element
Verify that subsequent operations (add/remove) work correctly after corruption

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Connector A creates token position, Connector B creates token position. Removing Connector A's position swaps Connector B's position but recomputes its key using calculatorConnector (accountingManager) instead of Connector B. The isPositionUsed mapping now has a stale entry for B's original key and a wrong entry at the new key. Future lookups for B's position fail, potentially causing duplicate position creation or inability to remove positions, bloating the holdingPositions array and corrupting TVL calculations.
prerequisites: Multiple holding positions from different connectors

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Corrupted position tracking leads to incorrect TVL calculations, potentially duplicating position value or preventing position cleanup
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vault depositors affected by incorrect TVL
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires manual registry repair or code fix

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for position removal with heterogeneous calculatorConnector values

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing holdingPositions array with isPositionUsed mapping

threats.title: AerodromeConnector._getPositionTVL Ignores Gauge-Staked LP Tokens, Understating TVL
threats.hypothesis_description: _getPositionTVL in AerodromeConnector only counts `IERC20(pool).balanceOf(address(this))` but the `stake()` function transfers LP tokens to the gauge contract, reducing the connector's balance to 0 for the staked portion. After staking, the Aerodrome position reports 0 TVL for all staked LP, systematically understating vault TVL. This deflates share price, allowing new depositors to acquire shares cheaply. When LP is later unstaked, TVL jumps, creating artificial profit.
threats.investigation_rationale: The stake() function moves LP to gauge, but _getPositionTVL doesn't query gauge.balanceOf(address(this)). This is a clear accounting gap between the deposit/stake flow and the TVL reporting flow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 balance = IERC20(pool).balanceOf(address(this)); — only counts unstaked LP, missing IGauge(gauge).balanceOf(address(this))

preconditions: AerodromeConnector has an active Aerodrome position
Manager has staked LP tokens into the gauge via stake()

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Only IERC20(pool).balanceOf(address(this)) queried, no gauge balance included
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After staking, balanceOf returns 0 for staked LP; gauge balance not queried
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No gauge balance query in TVL calculation

analysis_questions: What percentage of Aerodrome LP is typically staked in gauges?
How does the TVL drop affect share price calculations?
Can a manager exploit the TVL timing (stake->deposit->unstake) to benefit specific users?
validation_requirements: Stake LP tokens, then verify _getPositionTVL returns 0 for staked portion
Calculate share price impact before and after staking

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager stakes all Aerodrome LP into gauge. TVL drops by the full staked amount. New deposits receive more shares per token (deflated price). Manager unstakes, TVL recovers, inflating share value. Early depositors diluted; new depositors benefit from cheap shares.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Vault TVL understated by total value of staked LP; share price deflation proportional to Aerodrome position size
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Existing depositors diluted; new depositors gain unfair advantage
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix to include gauge balance

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for TVL after staking LP in gauge

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing on-chain gauge balance with reported TVL

threats.title: resetMiddle for Withdraw Queue Does Not Reset totalCBAmount, Enabling Double-Counting on Re-Calculation
threats.hypothesis_description: When resetMiddle is called for the withdraw queue, it moves withdrawQueue.middle backwards to allow re-calculation of withdrawal shares. However, calculateWithdrawShares accumulates assets into currentWithdrawGroup.totalCBAmount additively. Since totalCBAmount is NOT reset by resetMiddle, re-processing the same requests doubles the totalCBAmount. This leads to incorrect neededAssetsForWithdraw, potentially blocking fulfillment or causing wrong pro-rata distribution in executeWithdraw.
threats.investigation_rationale: resetMiddle only sets withdrawQueue.middle to a lower value but does not modify currentWithdrawGroup.totalCBAmount. calculateWithdrawShares uses `+=` to accumulate, so the same items are counted twice.

proof_of_signal.pattern: resetMiddle sets withdrawQueue.middle = newMiddle without resetting currentWithdrawGroup.totalCBAmount; calculateWithdrawShares at line 219 uses currentWithdrawGroup.totalCBAmount += assetsNeededForWithdraw

preconditions: Manager has already called calculateWithdrawShares for some withdrawal requests
Manager calls resetMiddle to move withdraw queue middle backwards
Manager calls calculateWithdrawShares again for the same requests

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: totalCBAmount += without reset when middle is moved backwards
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Re-calculated requests add to totalCBAmount that already includes their previous calculation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No totalCBAmount reset in resetMiddle; no check for already-calculated items

analysis_questions: What exact value does totalCBAmount reach after double-counting?
How does inflated totalCBAmount affect neededAssetsForWithdraw?
Does the pro-rata distribution in executeWithdraw over/under-pay withdrawers?
validation_requirements: Trace resetMiddle followed by re-calculation, verifying totalCBAmount doubles
Calculate impact on fulfillCurrentWithdrawGroup and executeWithdraw
Test whether the vault can recover from this state

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager calculates withdraw shares for 10 requests totaling 100 tokens. Manager calls resetMiddle to move middle back. Manager re-calculates the same 10 requests, now totalCBAmount = 200. When trying to fulfill, neededAssetsForWithdraw demands 200 tokens instead of 100, either blocking fulfillment or causing connectors to over-withdraw.
prerequisites: Manager has ability to call resetMiddle (onlyManager)
Existing calculated but unstarted withdraw requests

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inflated totalCBAmount causes incorrect asset retrieval demands and wrong pro-rata distribution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users with pending withdrawals in the affected group

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for resetMiddle followed by re-calculation

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Requires two manager transactions: resetMiddle then calculateWithdrawShares
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing totalCBAmount with sum of individual withdrawal amounts

threats.title: calculateWithdrawShares Callable During Fulfilled-But-Not-Executed State, Orphaning Calculated Amounts
threats.hypothesis_description: The withdraw group state machine allows calculateWithdrawShares to execute when currentWithdrawGroup.isStarted==true AND isFullfilled==true (the fulfilled-but-not-fully-executed state). The guard only blocks when isFullfilled==false AND isStarted==true. New calculations during this window add to totalCBAmount, but when executeWithdraw completes and deletes the withdraw group struct, these accumulated amounts are destroyed. The next withdraw group starts with totalCBAmount=0, missing the calculations done during this window.
threats.investigation_rationale: The guard condition `if (currentWithdrawGroup.isFullfilled == false && currentWithdrawGroup.isStarted == true) revert` explicitly allows the (true,true) state. Calculations in this window accumulate into totalCBAmount which is destroyed by `delete currentWithdrawGroup`.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Guard at line 206-208 only blocks (isStarted=true, isFullfilled=false); allows (true,true); delete at line 267 destroys orphaned totalCBAmount

preconditions: A withdraw group has been fulfilled but not fully executed (some items remain)
New withdrawal requests have been queued with recordTime <= oldestUpdateTime
Manager calls calculateWithdrawShares before completing executeWithdraw

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Guard allows calculateWithdrawShares when isStarted=true, isFullfilled=true
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Accumulated totalCBAmount destroyed by `delete currentWithdrawGroup` at line 267
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No guard for the (isStarted=true, isFullfilled=true) state

analysis_questions: What happens to calculated withdrawal requests whose amounts are lost?
Do the calculated requests get re-calculated in the next group?
Is there a path where users lose funds due to wrong pro-rata calculations?
validation_requirements: Simulate the sequence: fulfill -> calculateWithdrawShares -> executeWithdraw (completing) -> verify next group state
Check if withdrawQueue.middle advances past requests whose amounts are lost

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Manager fulfills a withdraw group. Before executing all withdrawals, manager calculates new withdrawal requests, advancing withdrawQueue.middle and adding to totalCBAmount. Manager completes executeWithdraw, which deletes the entire currentWithdrawGroup including the newly accumulated totalCBAmount. The next startCurrentWithdrawGroup has totalCBAmount=0 but lastId includes the recently-calculated requests, causing incorrect pro-rata distribution.
prerequisites: Active fulfilled withdraw group with pending execution
New withdrawal requests available for calculation

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect pro-rata distribution in subsequent withdraw groups; users may receive incorrect amounts
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users with withdrawals calculated during the vulnerable window
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires manager awareness and careful sequencing

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for calculateWithdrawShares during fulfilled-but-not-executed state

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Requires specific ordering of manager operations
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring totalCBAmount vs withdrawal queue state

threats.title: UNIv3Connector._getPositionTVL Uses Aggregate Pool Position Key, Potentially Overstating TVL
threats.hypothesis_description: UNIv3Connector._getPositionTVL computes the Uniswap V3 pool position key as `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU))`. In Uniswap V3, the pool tracks positions by `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(owner, tickLower, tickUpper))` where `owner` is the NFT position manager. This key returns the AGGREGATE liquidity of ALL positions managed by the NFT position manager at those tick ranges, not just the connector's specific NFT position. If other users have positions through the same NFT manager at identical ticks, the connector reports inflated TVL.
threats.investigation_rationale: The position key uses positionManager as owner, which is shared by all users of that NFT manager. The code should use the specific NFT tokenId to get individual position data, not the aggregate.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bytes32 key = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU)); pool.positions(key) returns aggregate liquidity for ALL positions at those ticks

preconditions: Other users have Uniswap V3 positions through the same NonfungiblePositionManager at identical tick ranges
UNIv3Connector has an active position at those ticks

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Pool position key uses positionManager as owner, returning aggregate for all NFTs at same ticks
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: liquidity, tokensOwed0, tokensOwed1 include other users' positions
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should use positionManager.positions(tokenId) to get per-NFT liquidity

analysis_questions: How likely is it that other users have positions at identical tick ranges?
What is the magnitude of potential TVL overstatement?
Can this be exploited by an attacker creating positions at the same ticks?
validation_requirements: Compare pool.positions(key) output with positionManager.positions(tokenId) for a specific NFT
Test with multiple positions at same tick range from different users

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker identifies the connector's tick range. Attacker creates their own large Uniswap V3 position at identical ticks through the same NonfungiblePositionManager. The connector's TVL calculation now includes the attacker's liquidity, inflating vault TVL. Attacker queues a withdrawal at the inflated share price, then removes their Uni V3 position.
prerequisites: Capital to create Uni V3 positions

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: TVL inflated by value of other users' positions at same ticks; share price artificially high
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vault depositors/withdrawers affected by inflated TVL
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix to use per-NFT position data

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test comparing aggregate vs per-NFT position data

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Attacker needs capital to create Uni V3 positions at same ticks
technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Multi-block: create position, wait for TVL calculation, withdraw
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing pool.positions(key) with NFT-specific data

threats.title: collectManagementFees Underflows When preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution Exceeds totalSupply Minus Fee Receiver Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: collectManagementFees computes `currentFeeShares = balanceOf(managementFeeReceiver) + balanceOf(performanceFeeReceiver) + preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution`, then subtracts this from `totalShares` to compute the fee base. However, `preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution` represents unminted shares (not yet in totalSupply). If this value is large enough, `totalShares - currentFeeShares` underflows in Solidity 0.8.20's checked arithmetic, causing a revert that blocks management fee collection entirely.
threats.investigation_rationale: preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution is set by recordProfitForFee via previewDeposit on the profit amount. This value is NOT reflected in totalSupply() until collectPerformanceFees mints them. The subtraction will revert if currentFeeShares > totalShares.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (totalShares - currentFeeShares) where currentFeeShares includes unminted preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution; checked arithmetic reverts on underflow

preconditions: Large profit has been recorded via recordProfitForFee
preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution is large relative to totalSupply
collectPerformanceFees has NOT yet been called to mint these shares

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: totalShares - currentFeeShares underflows when preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution > totalShares - balanceOf(mgmt) - balanceOf(perf)
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Unminted performance fee shares added to currentFeeShares but not to totalSupply
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No cap or check that currentFeeShares <= totalShares

analysis_questions: Under what profit/TVL ratios does preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution exceed the safe threshold?
How long can management fee collection be blocked?
Can an attacker intentionally trigger this by manipulating profit calculations?
validation_requirements: Calculate preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution for various profit scenarios
Test that collectManagementFees reverts when the value is large enough
Verify the window between recordProfitForFee and collectPerformanceFees

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Large vault profit is recorded. preformanceFeeSharesWaitingForDistribution becomes large (e.g., > totalSupply minus fee receiver balances). collectManagementFees reverts on underflow. Management fees cannot be collected for 12-48 hours until collectPerformanceFees mints the pending shares. If combined with TVL manipulation, this could be a persistent DoS.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Management fee collection blocked; potential accumulation of uncollected fees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Resolves after collectPerformanceFees is called; permanent if profit drops

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for collectManagementFees when large performance fee is pending

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: 12-48 hour window between recordProfitForFee and collectPerformanceFees
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring revert on collectManagementFees

threats.title: UniswapValueOracle Silently Truncates Amount to uint128, Causing Severe Price Underreporting
threats.hypothesis_description: UniswapValueOracle.getValue performs `uint128 amountIn128 = uint128(amount)` which silently truncates any amount exceeding uint128.max (~3.4e38). For tokens with very large total supplies or for high-precision internal calculations, this truncation returns an incorrect lower value. The truncated result is used in TVL calculations, understating the vault's total value and deflating share prices.
threats.investigation_rationale: The OracleLibrary.getQuoteAtTick function requires uint128 input. The conversion from uint256 to uint128 is implicit and unchecked, silently losing high bits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint128 amountIn128 = uint128(amount); — silent truncation of uint256 to uint128

preconditions: Token amount exceeds uint128.max in the oracle query
UniswapValueOracle is used as the price source for the token

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Amounts > 2^128 are silently reduced, returning wildly incorrect values

analysis_questions: Are there any real tokens where amounts could exceed uint128.max?
Could intermediate calculations in NoyaValueOracle routes produce amounts > uint128.max?
What is the worst-case TVL underreporting?
validation_requirements: Test getValue with amounts near and above uint128.max
Verify the truncation behavior produces incorrect output

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A token with very high decimals or large supply produces amounts > uint128.max in oracle queries. The truncated value is orders of magnitude smaller than the actual amount, causing severe TVL underreporting. Share prices drop, allowing new depositors to acquire shares at a steep discount.
prerequisites: Token amounts exceeding uint128.max in oracle queries

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Severe TVL underreporting; share price manipulation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix to add overflow check

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by providing large amounts to getValue

threats.title: ChainlinkOracleConnector Does Not Account for Feed Decimal Precision, Causing Incorrect Price Calculations
threats.hypothesis_description: ChainlinkOracleConnector.getValueFromChainlinkFeed computes `(amountIn * uintprice) / sourceTokenUnit` for non-inverse feeds. The `sourceTokenUnit` is `10^tokenDecimals` (the asset's ERC20 decimals), but the Chainlink price itself has its own decimal precision (typically 8 for USD feeds, 18 for ETH feeds), obtained via `source.decimals()`. This feed decimal is never queried or used in the calculation, meaning the result's precision depends on the Chainlink feed's internal decimal format rather than the expected base token precision. For cross-decimal pairs (e.g., 18-decimal token priced via 8-decimal feed into 6-decimal base), values are off by orders of magnitude.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard Chainlink integration requires normalizing output using the feed's decimals(). The code only uses the token's ERC20 decimals, not the feed's decimal count.

proof_of_signal.pattern: return (amountIn * uintprice) / (sourceTokenUnit) — sourceTokenUnit = 10^tokenDecimals, but feed.decimals() is never called or used

preconditions: Chainlink feed used has different decimals than expected by the formula
Asset and base token have different decimal precisions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Feed decimal precision (source.decimals()) not queried or incorporated into price calculation
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Result precision is feed-dependent, not normalized to base token decimals
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No call to source.decimals() for normalization

analysis_questions: For which token pairs does the precision mismatch cause material errors?
Does the NoyaValueOracle routing compensate for this in practice?
What is the actual output for a concrete example (e.g., USDC/ETH with 8-decimal feed)?
validation_requirements: Trace a complete price calculation for a real token pair
Compare output with expected value normalized to base token decimals
Test with multiple feed decimal formats (8, 18)

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: When querying the value of a token via a Chainlink feed whose decimal format doesn't match the assumed formula, the returned value is orders of magnitude wrong. If overstated, TVL inflates, share price rises artificially; if understated, TVL deflates. Either direction enables economic exploitation through deposits/withdrawals at incorrect prices.
prerequisites: Specific Chainlink feed decimal format not matching formula assumptions

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: TVL off by orders of magnitude for affected token pairs
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vault depositors/withdrawers when affected tokens are in holdings
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires code fix to incorporate feed decimals

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test with varied Chainlink feed decimal formats

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing oracle output with known prices

threats.title: Flash Loan Address Has Full Manager Access Across All Vaults and Connectors
threats.hypothesis_description: The onlyManager modifier in NoyaGovernanceBase grants access to `registry.flashLoan()` — a single global address that can call ALL manager-restricted functions across ALL vaults and connectors. This includes executeDeposit, executeWithdraw, calculateShares, fee collection, swap operations, and connector strategy functions. If the flash loan address is set to a contract like the Balancer Vault that allows arbitrary callbacks during flash loans, any user could gain manager-level access during a flash loan callback.
threats.investigation_rationale: The onlyManager modifier explicitly checks `msg.sender == registry.flashLoan()`. The flash loan address is a global setting changed via MAINTAINER_ROLE. Deployment script sets it to address(0), but setFlashLoanAddress can change it.
deployment_assumptions: flashLoan address is set to a flash loan provider that allows arbitrary callbacks (e.g., Balancer Vault, Aave)
threats.config_evidence: deploySharedContracts.ts sets flashLoan to 0x0000000000000000000000000000000000000000 at deployment; setFlashLoanAddress can change it later
affected_functions: All onlyManager functions in AccountingManager and connectors

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.sender == registry.flashLoan() bypasses keeper/emergency check in onlyManager

preconditions: flashLoan address is set to a contract that allows arbitrary callbacks (not address(0))
The flash loan provider does not restrict what the callback can call

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: registry.flashLoan() granted same access as keeperContract and emergencyManager
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Flash loan providers typically allow arbitrary callbacks where msg.sender = provider
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No scope restriction on what flashLoan address can do; same access as full manager

analysis_questions: What flash loan provider is intended to be used?
Does the provider allow arbitrary callbacks (making msg.sender the provider)?
Can an attacker use a flash loan callback to call onlyManager functions?
validation_requirements: Verify flash loan provider callback mechanism
Test calling onlyManager functions from within a flash loan callback
Assess whether the flash loan address is ever set to a non-zero value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If flashLoan is set to a protocol like Balancer Vault, an attacker initiates a flash loan. During the callback, msg.sender is the Balancer Vault (same as registry.flashLoan()). The attacker calls any onlyManager function: executeWithdraw to drain funds, resetMiddle to corrupt queues, or swapHoldings to swap assets to attacker-controlled addresses.
prerequisites: flashLoan set to a flash loan provider with unrestricted callbacks

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete vault drainage across all vaults; manager-level operations executable by any address
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All vault depositors across all vaults
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires emergency pause; stolen funds unrecoverable

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test for flash loan callback exploiting onlyManager

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring unexpected onlyManager calls from flashLoan address

threats.title: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity Is Empty No-Op, Providing Zero Withdrawal Verification
threats.hypothesis_description: Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity is declared as `function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { }` — a completely empty function body. BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress calls this function when the AccountingManager requests token withdrawal from a connector, but since it does nothing, any withdrawal amount passes validation. The Watcher role is documented as a critical security mechanism to "undo actions if misbehavior is detected," but this function provides zero protection.
threats.investigation_rationale: The empty function body means the Watcher verification step in the withdrawal flow provides no security guarantee. The protocol documentation explicitly lists Watchers as a monitoring/reverting role.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { } — empty body

preconditions: Compromised keeper/manager initiates withdrawal from connector to AccountingManager
Watcher verification is the intended safeguard against excessive withdrawals

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Empty function body providing no verification
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress relies on this for withdrawal validation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No amount comparison, no signature verification, no threshold checks

analysis_questions: Is this intentionally left empty for future implementation?
Are there off-chain Watcher mechanisms that compensate?
What would a proper implementation look like?
validation_requirements: Confirm that any withdrawal amount passes through sendTokensToTrustedAddress unchecked
Verify that Watchers is indeed the multi-sig contract used as watcherContract in vault config

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised keeper calls retrieveTokensForWithdraw with inflated amounts. The connector's sendTokensToTrustedAddress calls Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity which does nothing. Arbitrary amounts of tokens are transferred from connector to AccountingManager, where the keeper can then manipulate withdrawal execution to extract funds.
prerequisites: Compromised keeper/manager with ability to call retrieveTokensForWithdraw

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Entire connector balance extractable without Watcher validation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Emergency pause can stop further damage; extracted funds may be recoverable if still in AccountingManager

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing test verifying Watcher actually validates withdrawal amounts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Off-chain monitoring of unusual withdrawal patterns
